steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: The blank joins two independent clauses: ‘Compiling isn’t exclusive to certain programming languages’ and ‘any language can incorporate this tool,’ with ‘however’ marking a contrast. Two valid placements exist for the conjunctive adverb: ending IC1 as a trailing parenthetical (‘, however;’) or starting IC2 (‘; however,’). Test which placement the sentence prefers.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘languages; however,’: Grammatically valid — semicolon joins the two ICs, ‘however’ opens IC2 with a following comma. But this reading frames the contrast as a fresh announcement (‘however, any language can...’), which slightly misplaces the emphasis: the point being qualified is the claim just made about exclusivity, not the new statement about any language. The sentence semantically wants ‘however’ to close off the first assertion. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘languages, however,’: Comma before and comma after ‘however’ leaves only commas separating two independent clauses — a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘languages. However,’: Creates two separate sentences. The passage presents a single unified contrast and the blank is within a continuous sentence; splitting into two fragments misrepresents that structure. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘languages, however;’: The comma binds ‘however’ to IC1 as a trailing parenthetical sentence-adverb, softening the claim that compiling belongs to certain languages. The semicolon then performs the standard independent-clause join before IC2. Parse: [IC1 + trailing ‘however’]; [IC2]. This places the contrast exactly where the sentence intends it — qualifying the first assertion before moving to the second. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
